title: Large PDF attachments corrupted on download — size-dependent regression introduced ~1 week ago
problem: PDF files attached to tasks are downloaded in a corrupted/unreadable state. The corruption is size-dependent: small single-page PDFs work correctly, while larger multi-page PDFs (10-20 pages) consistently fail. The PDF viewer reports the file as damaged or not a valid PDF. This is a regression — uploads were working correctly until approximately one week ago. Multiple customers are affected and the support team is fielding daily complaints.
root_cause_hypothesis: A change deployed approximately one week ago likely introduced a file-size-dependent corruption in the upload or download pipeline. Prime suspects: (1) a chunk size or buffer limit change in the upload handler that truncates or corrupts files above a threshold, (2) a misconfigured multipart upload boundary, (3) a streaming/encoding change (e.g., incorrect Content-Transfer-Encoding or premature stream termination), or (4) a storage layer change (e.g., S3 multipart upload configuration, CDN caching rule) that corrupts large objects.
reproduction_steps: Upload a small (1-page, <1 MB) PDF to a task — confirm it downloads and opens correctly
Upload a larger (10-20 page, likely >5 MB) PDF to a task
Download the larger PDF from the task
Attempt to open the downloaded file — expect 'file is damaged' or 'not a valid PDF' error
Compare the byte size of the uploaded original to the downloaded file to determine if truncation is occurring
environment: Production environment. No specific browser/OS reported — likely affects all clients since the corruption occurs server-side during upload or storage. Regression window: approximately one week prior to report (late March / early April 2026).
impact: Multiple customers are unable to use PDF attachments for larger documents, which is a core workflow for the support team. The support team is handling daily escalations from angry customers. No workaround exists for affected files — users cannot upload large PDFs at all.
recommended_fix: 1. Review deployment history for changes made approximately one week ago, focusing on file upload/download handlers, storage configuration, and any infrastructure changes (CDN, proxy, storage backend). 2. Compare the byte content of an uploaded large PDF against the stored version and the downloaded version to identify where corruption is introduced (upload path vs. storage vs. download path). 3. Check for truncation — if the downloaded file is smaller than the original, look for buffer size limits, chunk size changes, or premature stream closure. 4. If file sizes match, inspect for encoding issues (binary vs. base64 mismatches, Content-Type handling). 5. Check server logs for errors or warnings during large file uploads in the past week.
proposed_test_case: Upload PDFs of varying sizes (1 MB, 5 MB, 10 MB, 25 MB) and verify that each downloaded file is byte-identical to the original. Include a regression test that uploads a multi-page PDF above the observed failure threshold and asserts the downloaded content matches via checksum comparison.
information_gaps: Exact file size threshold where corruption begins (can be determined via internal testing)
Whether the downloaded file size differs from the original (truncation vs. encoding corruption — can be checked server-side)
Exact error message from PDF viewers (minor — corruption type can be determined by inspecting the file bytes)
Whether the stored file on the backend is already corrupted or only the downloaded copy (determines upload-path vs. download-path issue)
